title: Lesson 198
transcript: All right, we've been talking about the importance of applications and how they need to be secured. And one of the most important environments where we rely on applications to be able to give functionality is the database environment. Now, if you look at the history of databases, you know they've been around for decades and what they really allow us to do is store very sensitive valuable information that ultimately drives business decisions I mean that's why companies store information in databases. Now it is applications that allow us to interface with ultimately that data that is stored in the databases themselves. So when you think about how we protect any architecture, and we've talked about this many times before, The way that we protect architectures is by breaking them down into components, protecting each of the components. So when you look at a database architecture it's usually made up of different components such as the ones that you see here on the slide here. Obviously you've got hardware and on top of that you've got an operating system. And it is software or an application that really allows people to be able to interface with the database and its contents ultimately. And of course the whole important thing that we have to do in any environment here is to protect the data itself. but how we interface with the data ultimately is provided through the application itself. That means that the application has to have really good security. Now before we can talk about the security that's built into applications, we have to look at how database environments have really evolved over the years. And so ultimately what they look like today are typically tables. Tables that will represent information in two dimensional tables. In fact, in some cases, We don't even call them databases anymore, we call them tables and what we're talking about here is a technology called relational database management systems. The database management system is really the set of applications that allow users to interface with the contents of the database so that we can report and query and analyze to be able to drive decisions themselves. But what you see on the screen here is a representation of what relational databases actually look like. Two dimensional tables that allow us to structure data very easily But the reason why it's called relational is because we have the ability to be able to link and relate different tables together. And the advantage of that of course is to be able to analyze more information together. So if you put information together, we are able to use analysis tools to be able to look for trends and relationships and correlations and patterns that become evident because that information is together in how we link them for example and so the advancements that we've made for example in relation is that we use also different terminology to be able to relate to certain things that we used to call them before like for example they were called databases We try and not use the word database anymore. We actually call them tables or relations. And in fact, instead of calling them fields, in some cases in this technology, we call them attributes. and the records for example are typically referred to as tuples or tuples and the intersection of tuples and attributes is what we call for example a field. This is the terminology that we typically use in relational technology. Now the advantage of being able to use relational is where that name relational comes from. We can relate tables together but the only way that we can do this is to actually repeat information from one table into the other. And so the way that this typically works and we've got a pretty good example here that you see up on your screen. If we have a student table there is something called a primary key that is actually represented for example as the first field The student ID here is referred to as the primary key. Now that information has to be repeated into another table somewhere else. in order to allow that relation or that join to actually exist. So the primary key that exists in one table that is actually repeated in another table is referred to as the foreign key in that other table. Now there are some very important integrity rules. that would allow this to be done properly, because if you don't address those integrity rules, then obviously this link, this relation, is not something that's gonna happen properly. And so the two very important integrity rules that we need to remember are, first of all, entity integrity. And what that means is that for every primary key value that we have, for example in the student table, that value has to be valid. In other words, if you look at how it's structured right now it is always five characters but they are always numeric characters and of course if we added one more record into that student table you know the primary key in that record would have to be something that is unique. So through data validation we would ensure that we have entity integrity. That's what means having for example a valid primary key in that student table. the foreign key which is really the primary key that is repeated in the other table also has to be valid and if it is valid it allows the second integrity rule that we have to remember and that's called referential integrity. So referential integrity means that for every foreign key value, for example, that exists in the registration table, there's a valid reference back into the student table so that the two can actually be joined. linked and so the security that's built into the application would first of all ensure entity integrity every primary key is valid and referential integrity which means that for every foreign key that we have there's a valid link back into another table as a primary key for example. So that's what those two really important integrity rules mean. Now in any database environment, a database is only valuable if we update, for example, the information in the database to make it more factual, more relevant, more timely, more current. And so if you think about what databases look today, we have to allow a whole bunch of users to be able to update, for example, the records in that database at the same time. So what also becomes very important to address from a security perspective in any database environment are what we call concurrency controls, or some people refer to them as lock controls. And what this means is that we have to prevent, for example, two people from being able to make a change to the same database element at the same time because if we did obviously that would affect the integrity of the database contents itself and so we have to address what's called concurrency controls or law controls and what that means is applying what we call the ACID test which is what you see here up on your screen. Obviously the word ACID comes from the first letter of each of these requirements that need to be applied to any database that allows updating in real time for example. So we need to understand each one of these. The A is for atomicity and what that means is that either all changes take effect or none do. In other words, when you apply a transaction to a database, it typically requires a number of steps for us to go through before we can complete that transaction successfully. If your steps don't complete successfully, all of them, then the ones that you did complete successfully would have to be backed out. It would be atomicity controls within the application that would ensure that. And this is the one that we typically see a lot, especially online. You know, if you're making, for example, an update to a database, let's say you're booking a seat on a flight. and you're going through the process of booking that seat on a flight through your airline's website, for example, there's probably a number of steps that you have to go through before you complete that. Let's say you get to the point where you need to pay for it with your credit card. and your phone rings and you start talking on the phone, your conversation lasts, let's say 10 minutes, and then you hang up and you try and resume the transaction where you left it. The system is probably going to tell you that you've timed out. And so you need to start back at the beginning again. So all of those steps that you had completed up until that point where you got to the point where you were paying for it and you got that phone call would have to be backed out. It is atomicity controls that would ensure that. The second rule is consistency and simply what that means is that once you apply a transaction successfully to the database, that transaction that you've applied does not affect the integrity of the database before it looked like before you applied your transaction. In other words, by you booking a seat on a flight and doing it properly did not affect anyone else that also had seats on that same flight. In other words, your change does not adversely affect the integrity of the database before it looked like you made your change. The next one is isolation. And that simply means that the results of your transaction is not actually visible to any other user of the database. until you complete your transaction successfully. So nobody knows for example that you're booking the last business class seat because if they did they might try and get ahead of you. So everything that you're doing is isolated from any other user of the database until you complete that transaction successfully. It is isolation controls that would ensure that. And the last one, durability, I think pretty intuitive as far as what that means. If you apply a transaction successfully, that transaction lasts. It's durable. There's no way for it to disappear. when you show up to the airport to get on your flight there actually is a seat for you on that flight and so that's durability and when you put all of these together It means that the application that controls access to the database ensures that each of these requirements that are part of what we call the asset test are addressed properly and properly for us to ensure the integrity of the data within the database itself.
